title: Cat Scratch on Eyeball: Immediate Emergency Guide
meta_desc: A cat scratch on the eye is a high-urgency veterinary emergency. Learn about corneal injuries and why immediate professional care is required.
content_html:
What is a Corneal Injury?

A scratch directly on the eyeball is known medically as a corneal ulcer or a corneal laceration. The cornea is the clear, outer layer of the eye that acts as a window. Because it is highly sensitive and thin, even a minor scratch from a cat's claw can penetrate the layers, causing intense pain and risking the structural integrity of the globe.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. This is a high-urgency emergency. Eye injuries are notoriously unpredictable and can worsen within hours. A scratch can introduce bacteria deep into the eye, leading to rapid infection, 'melting' of the cornea, or permanent blindness. You should contact an emergency veterinarian or a veterinary ophthalmologist immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear, high-resolution photo to your vet during your initial call can be life-saving. A photo allows the triage team to see if there is a 'perforation' (a hole through the eye), an 'aqueous flare' (inflammation inside the eye), or a visible flap of tissue. This helps them prepare the necessary equipment and prioritize your pet the moment you walk through the door.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my pet?
faqs.answer: No. Never use human medications or over-the-counter eye drops on a scratched eyeball. Some ingredients, like steroids, can cause the cornea to dissolve if an ulcer is present.

faqs.question: How can I protect the eye until I get to the vet?
faqs.answer: Place an Elizabethan collar (cone) on your pet immediately to prevent them from pawing or rubbing the eye, which can turn a minor scratch into a rupture.

faqs.question: What are the signs that the scratch is severe?
faqs.answer: Signs of high severity include the eye looking cloudy or blue, visible blood inside the eye, a change in the pupil shape, or the pet being unable to open the eye at all.
